text: To give the benefit of the doubt
They can't have meant any harm.
He might have been confused by the custom.
They can't have meant it rudely.
The card must have been offered with one hand.
When I gave him my card, he went silent. I must have done something wrong.
Don't jump to conclusions. He might have just been surprised by the custom.
You think? I worried I'd come across as rude. It was such an awkward silence.
He can't have thought you meant any harm. You were clearly a polite visitor.
True. It must have been a small culture clash. I'll give him the benefit of the doubt.
Exactly. A smile next time will smooth it over. No one loses face over a card.
It must have been a culture clash.
I'll give them the benefit of the doubt.
A smile usually smooths things over.

Something funny happened on my last trip, and I still think about what it really meant. I was in Japan, having dinner with a colleague I had just met. I handed him my business card with one hand, the way I always do, and he went completely quiet. There was this long, awkward silence. At the time I thought, oh no, I must have said something wrong, or he must have taken offence at something. I read the whole situation as a disaster. But looking back now, with what I know, he can't have been angry. He was far too polite for that. He might have simply been surprised, because in Japan you offer a card with both hands, as a sign of respect. I had no idea. I jumped to conclusions, when really it was just a small culture clash. He must have realized I was a foreigner who meant no harm, because a minute later he smiled and we carried on. So now, when someone abroad goes quiet on me, I try to read between the lines. They might have a different custom. They can't have meant to be rude. I give them the benefit of the doubt, and nine times out of ten, the misunderstanding just melts away.

Hey Pricila, it's me. You asked me about that strange moment you had in Japan, so let me explain, because honestly, you can't have known the rule. It's a tiny custom, but it matters a lot over there. When you give someone your business card, you're supposed to hold it with both hands and a small bow, and the other person reads it carefully before putting it away. If you hand it over with one hand, the way we do back home, the other person might think you're being a little casual. But here's the thing: nobody who knows you would ever think you meant to be rude. He must have understood straight away that you were a visitor, and visitors can't have learned every local rule. That's just a faux pas, the kind every traveller makes. My advice? When you're abroad and someone reacts in a way you don't expect, don't assume the worst. Body language and customs change from place to place. People rarely mean any harm. Read between the lines, give them the benefit of the doubt, and a smile usually smooths everything over. You handled it just fine. Talk soon.

First, Pricila bows to a colleague in Japan, and he looks surprised. Then she worries: she must have done something wrong. Next, a friend explains the local custom, so she realizes she can't have known the rule. After that, she sees he meant no harm, and she gives him the benefit of the doubt. So she smooths things over with a warm smile, and the awkward silence melts away.
